issues.title: [Dependabot] GHSA-pph8-gcv7-4qj5 in pyo3
issues.description:
## PyO3 Risk of buffer overflow in `PyString::from_object`

**Provider:** GitHub Dependabot
**Repository:** `AnthusAI/Virtuus`
**Alert Number:** 2
**Severity:** low
**State:** open
**Package:** `pyo3`
**Ecosystem:** `rust`
**Manifest:** `rust/Cargo.lock`

### Advisory
`PyString::from_object` took `&str` arguments and forwarded them directly to the Python C API without checking for terminating nul bytes. This could lead the Python interpreter to read beyond the end of the `&str` data and potentially leak contents of the out-of-bounds read (by raising a Python exception containing a copy of the data including the overflow).

In PyO3 0.24.1 this function will now allocate a `CString` to guarantee a terminating nul bytes. PyO3 0.25 will likely offer an alternative API which takes `&CStr` arguments.

### Reference
- https://github.com/AnthusAI/Virtuus/security/dependabot/2

issues.title: Rust PyO3 extension build fails (maturin develop)

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Repro
From repo root:
- `cd rust`
- `/opt/anaconda3/bin/conda run -n virtuus maturin develop --release`

## Result
Build fails with multiple compile errors in `rust/src/python.rs` (PyO3 0.21 API changes / borrow issues). Examples:
- E0282 type annotations needed (depythonize on `junction.as_ref(py)`)
- E0599 `borrow` not found for `&Py<PyList>` (should use bound/as_ref)
- E0596 cannot borrow `table` as mutable (missing `mut`)
- E0716 temporary value dropped while borrowed (`fk_value.to_string()` inline)
- E0502 mutable/immutable borrow conflict in `table.association_defs()` + `table.scan()`

## Impact
Blocks Rust backend import (`virtuus._rust`) and therefore Rust-side benchmarks via Python harness.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Progress (2026-02-23)
Implemented fixes in `rust/src/python.rs` to restore PyO3 build compatibility:
- fixed mutable borrows (`export`, `describe`, `validate`)
- resolved borrow conflicts in `validate` by cloning defs and scanning once
- avoided temporary borrow issues for FK strings
- updated PyDict `get_item` handling to account for PyResult
- fixed `scan` to collect pythonized results without type mismatch
- switched junction depythonize to `depythonize_bound` using `into_bound`
- updated hook iteration to use `bind`

`cargo check --features python` now passes (warnings only), and `maturin develop --release` succeeds. `virtuus._rust` import verified.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text: Done: PyO3 build fixed; maturin develop --release succeeds and virtuus._rust imports. Next: close issue. Blockers: none.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Perf improvement options
- **Profile hot paths (Rust):** perf/flamegraph on GSI query + PK lookup; focus on clones/allocs in `table.rs` (Vec sorting, serde_json Value handling).
- **Index structures:** use `BTreeMap` per GSI partition to keep sorted order without per-query sort; consider `FxHashMap` for PK map to reduce hashing cost.
- **Serde overhead:** try typed structs or borrowing `Value`/`Cow` to avoid clones on hot paths.
- **I/O batching:** reuse buffers and `BufWriter` for export/persistence; reduce syscalls.
- **Parallel load:** rayon-based directory load + GSI build per partition to speed cold load.
- **Query caching:** optional per-GSI partition cache of last sorted slice for repeated scans.
- **Instrumentation:** add tracing spans around load/query to split time between sort vs deserialization.

Next: run perf on 100k corpus, prototype BTreeMap GSI, measure vs baseline.

issues.title: Rust performance investigation --status in_progress
issues.description:
## Goal
Make Rust cold-load meaningfully faster than Python for record loading; target up to ~10x if feasible.

## Context
Rust version currently performs similar to Python, which is unexpected. We need to leverage Rust for speed (e.g., parallel load) and empirically validate improvements.

## Approach
For each performance idea: implement, ensure specs pass, benchmark vs Rust baseline and Python, and keep only if gains justify complexity. If not, revert to baseline and record what was tried/learned.

## Tracking
Create one story per idea with separate notes; work may use Git worktrees for isolation.

comments.author: ryan.porter

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Conclusion (2026-02-23)
The benchmark harness is Python-based. When `VIRTUUS_BENCH_BACKEND=rust`, it tries to import `virtuus._rust`; if that import fails it silently falls back to the Python backend.

On this machine, the Rust PyO3 extension currently **does not build**, so `virtuus._rust` is not importable. As a result, attempts to run “Rust” benchmarks are actually exercising the Python backend. This explains why historical results show Rust and Python with equivalent performance.

Evidence:
- `maturin develop --release` fails with compile errors in `rust/src/python.rs` (PyO3 API/borrow issues) → `virtuus._rust` missing.
- Benchmark JSON does not record backend; Python harness defaults to Python when Rust import fails.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Progress (2026-02-23)
- Fixed PyO3 build issues in `rust/src/python.rs`; `maturin develop --release` now succeeds and `virtuus._rust` imports.
- Captured 10k yardstick baselines:
  - Python full_database_cold_load: 3354.440 ms
  - Rust full_database_cold_load: 2929.900 ms

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Rust-native benchmark update (2026-02-23)
Ran Rust directly via CLI (no Python harness). `cargo run --release --bin virtuus -- query` with schema loading all tables, 10k dataset. Mean load+query time: **2807.248 ms** (3 runs). Details logged on task virt-4a074a.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Experiment result: Parallel IO (Rayon)
Tried parallelizing `Table::load_from_dir` with Rayon (read+parse in parallel, sequential put). Benchmarks at 10k `full_database_cold_load` showed **no meaningful improvement** (mean ~2934 ms vs prior ~2930 ms). Changes were reverted and dependency removed.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Experiment result: byte reads + preallocation
Tried `fs::read` + `serde_json::from_slice` with pre-allocation in `Table::load_from_dir`. 10k `full_database_cold_load` showed no meaningful improvement (median ~2884 ms, mean worse due outlier). Reverted change.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Win: Load is now read-only
Changed `load_from_dir` to skip disk writes during load (Python + Rust), aligning with README (only `put`/`delete` write). 10k `full_database_cold_load` improved from ~2.9–3.3s to ~0.48s (mean Rust 478.758 ms, Python 492.872 ms). Keeping change.

issues.title: [Dependabot] GHSA-mc8h-8q98-g5hr in remove_dir_all
issues.description:
## Race Condition Enabling Link Following and Time-of-check Time-of-use (TOCTOU) Race Condition in remove_dir_all

**Provider:** GitHub Dependabot
**Repository:** `AnthusAI/Virtuus`
**Alert Number:** 1
**Severity:** low
**State:** open
**Package:** `remove_dir_all`
**Ecosystem:** `rust`
**Manifest:** `rust/Cargo.lock`

### Advisory
The `remove_dir_all` crate is a Rust library that offers additional features over the Rust standard library `fs::remove_dir_all` function. It suffers the same class of failure as the code it was layering over: TOCTOU race conditions, with the ability to cause arbitrary paths to be deleted by substituting a symlink for a path after the type of the path was checked.

Thanks to the Rust security team for identifying the problem and alerting us to it.

### Reference
- https://github.com/AnthusAI/Virtuus/security/dependabot/1

comments.author: derek.norrbom
comments.text: Investigated CI failure after release automation change (run 22876496241). Root cause is version skew introduced by semantic-release commit 299fa2c: rust/Cargo.toml and python/pyproject.toml were bumped to 0.3.0, but root VERSION remained 0.2.2. Python smoke path reads __version__ from VERSION file, so scenario 'Python and Rust report the same version' fails with Python 0.2.2 vs Rust 0.3.0.

comments.author: derek.norrbom
comments.text: Implementing best-practice fix: update semantic-release configuration to use supported version file tracking key so root VERSION is updated on every release, and align current VERSION value with released 0.3.0 to restore Python/Rust version parity smoke test.

comments.author: derek.norrbom
comments.text: Implemented fix in branch: switched [tool.semantic_release] from version_files to version_variables with plain-file format (VERSION:*:nf), and pinned CI install to python-semantic-release~=10.0 to reduce breaking config drift. Validated pyproject parses and semantic-release CLI runs without config errors.

comments.author: derek.norrbom
comments.text: Prepared operator steps for one-time VERSION repair via PR-only flow (no direct main push). Verified current remote skew persists on origin/main: VERSION=0.2.2 while python/rust manifest versions are 0.3.0.

comments.author: derek.norrbom
comments.text: Root cause verified via reproduction with python-semantic-release 10.5.3 debug logs: version_variables entry  compiles to a variable-assignment regex and records num_matches=0 on file VERSION, so the plain file value is never updated. Release 0.3.1 updated python/pyproject.toml + rust/Cargo.toml only; VERSION stayed 0.3.0, causing CI mismatch Python(0.3.0 from VERSION) vs Rust(0.3.1 from Cargo).

comments.author: derek.norrbom

Release 0.3.1 updated python/pyproject.toml + rust/Cargo.toml only; VERSION stayed 0.3.0, causing CI mismatch Python(0.3.0 from VERSION) vs Rust(0.3.1 from Cargo).

comments.author: derek.norrbom
comments.text: Implementing durable fix: convert VERSION file to explicit assignment syntax compatible with python-semantic-release version_variables, set precise variable key in config, and update Python/Rust VERSION readers in smoke paths to parse semver token robustly. This removes recurring mismatch risk in future releases.

comments.author: derek.norrbom
comments.text: Analyzed duplicate Release runs 22918805264 (success) and 22919016292 (failure). Root cause: second run triggered by release commit itself; semantic-release version reported "No release will be made", but tag detection still marked released=true because HEAD already had v0.4.0 tag, causing duplicate publish attempts and crates.io "already exists" failure. Applying workflow guard so publish steps run only when this run created a new release.

comments.author: derek.norrbom
comments.text: Verified duplicate release behavior from Actions logs: first release run (head 475e230) succeeded, second run (head e3b4e99) was triggered by anthusbot and attempted duplicate publish. Applying release.yml hardening to skip bot-triggered workflow_run and gate publish steps on whether semantic-release version creates a new tag in this run.

comments.author: derek.norrbom
comments.text: Applied release workflow hardening: (1) skip release job when workflow_run actor is anthusbot, (2) record tags before/after semantic-release version and set semrel_version.outputs.released only when a new tag appears, and (3) gate semantic-release publish + PyPI + crates publish on that output. This prevents duplicate publish attempts on bot-triggered reruns.

comments.author: derek.norrbom
comments.text: Verified persistent Cargo.lock churn root cause: rust/Cargo.toml on main is 0.4.1 but committed rust/Cargo.lock is still 0.4.0. Any cargo metadata invocation rewrites lock package version to 0.4.1, causing recurring dirty tree/pull friction. Recommended durable fix: PR that commits rust/Cargo.lock synchronized to 0.4.1.

comments.author: derek.norrbom
comments.text: Diagnosed additional post-release failure: separate workflow .github/workflows/publish-pypi.yml auto-triggers on release:published and attempts a second PyPI publish. Its OIDC claims map to publish-pypi.yml@refs/tags/*, causing invalid-publisher under current trusted publisher settings and duplicate publish intent. Applying fix: make publish-pypi workflow manual-only (workflow_dispatch) to keep a single automatic publishing path in release.yml.

comments.author: derek.norrbom
comments.text:
Post-remediation check (2026-03-02):

Implemented hardening changes:
- Replaced string-prefix path checks with robust pathlib containment checks (`relative_to`-based) in:
  - `tools/pull_cold_results_s3.py`
  - `tools/pull_storage_results_s3.py`
  - `examples/sakila/build_sakila.py`
- Tightened Sakila DB download URL policy in `examples/sakila/build_sakila.py`:
  - parse with `urlparse`
  - require `https`
  - restrict host to allowlist (`sq.io` default + optional `--allow-db-host`)

Validation:
- `python3 -m black` on touched files: pass
- `python3 -m ruff check` on touched files: pass
- Re-ran `snyk code test`: still 16 medium findings

Interpretation:
- SSRF finding was removed after host allowlist change.
- Remaining findings are still concentrated in developer CLI path flows and list-form subprocess usage. Snyk continues to report PT/Command Injection even after containment checks, indicating these are largely static-analysis false positives for this repo context.

Recommendation now:
- Keep the code hardening changes (they improve real safety).
- Dismiss remaining findings in Snyk with explicit rationale (developer-controlled CLI/tooling context + non-shell subprocess + containment guards).
- Reopen/annotate previously closed related sub-tasks to note that Snyk still reports at scan level despite mitigations.
